decision_summary: The chip temperature (89.16°C) exceeds the 85.0°C limit. Previous attempts to maximize spreader width hit geometry bounds, and conductivity was incorrectly pushed beyond its 500.0 W/(m*K) upper limit. Since the spreader material is already at its maximum allowed conductivity (500.0), further material tuning there is impossible. The next most effective lever is to increase the base plate conductivity to improve heat extraction from the spreader to the cooled boundaries. I will increase base_material.conductivity from 12.0 to 24.0 (2x step, within bounds [5.0, 250.0]).

changes.reason: Spreader conductivity is already at max (500.0). Increasing base plate conductivity improves the thermal path from the spreader to the Dirichlet boundaries (left/right walls at 25°C), helping to lower the overall system resistance and reduce chip peak temperature.

expected_effects: Reduction in chip_max_temperature towards the 85.0°C constraint
Improved heat flux transfer from the high-conductivity spreader into the base plate
Flattening of the temperature gradient across the assembly
risk_notes: Diminishing returns: The base plate is not the primary spreading layer, so the temperature drop may be smaller than direct spreader improvements.
If this single step is insufficient, subsequent iterations may need to explore geometric repositioning (components.2.x0) to better align the spreader with the cooling boundaries.
Ensure the 2x multiplier stays within the allowed single-step ratio constraint.
